title: Level 808
wikitext:
{{Blurbackground|file=Eyebg.jpg}} {{User:JustASpecificGuy/BlueCSS}} {{CSS|User:Lancelot_Chen/Animations.css}} {{CSS|content= @import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css?family=Marcellus'); /* Font */ html .resizable-container, .page-content h1, .page-content h2, .page-content h3, .page-content h4, .page-content h5, .page-content h6 { font-family: 'Marcellus'}} {{DISPLAYTITLE:Calm at the Center of Everything}}
I didn’t run when the warnings came, screaming and shouting at me on my TV as the screen turned a blood red danger. I simply accepted it. The radio screamed evacuation orders. Neighbors packed their cars, their faces filled with fear for their lives. But I sat on my porch, watching the sky bruise purple, and waited for my time. The old man living next door calmly strolled up to me, dragging along a suitcase in his left hand. "Boy, the storm is coming soon. You should hurry yourself out of here." I shook my head. I looked behind me at my sparsely decorated living room, then turned back around to respond. "No, I don't think I will. God may be forgiving, but I am not. You should leave, and don't dawdle now." The old man gave me an unreadable expression before walking back to his car, where his son was frantically shoving items into the trunk. He gave one final look back at me, as if out of pity, before slamming the door shut, the car screeching down the road and into the horizon. I rolled my shoulders back and thought about retribution. I was never a perfect man. My mother loathed my sloth and my wife only barely tolerated it. I wouldn't be of help around the house, and would often be out at the local bar drinking my consciousness away. It was on one of those days my house was robbed. I should have been there to protect her. I should have been there to save her. The funeral was a small one, and I cried for days. The only person who truly loved me was gone. I had nothing left to live for now. When the hurricane hit, it didn’t feel like punishment. It felt like absolution. The wind tore the roofs from houses, the rain swallowed the streets, and I stood in the doorway of my crumbling home, laughing as the storm ruthlessly ripped the roof off and took me. Strong gusts of wind slapped my face as I felt myself being lift up into the air. After long I was ten feet in the air, flying away from my troubles and a world that hated me with a passion. I felt myself losing consciousness as I accepted my fate. The last thing I saw before the darkness swallowed everything was the church steeple snapping in half—ironic, considering. Then—silence. I woke to sunlight. The town was still here. Not ruined. Not saved. Just stopped. The hurricane hadn’t passed. It loomed above me, a monstrous wall of pristine white clouds circling endlessly, but here, in the eye, the air was dead calm. The streets were empty. No bodies. No ghosts. Just me. I groaned as I sat up off the soft bed of grass I had landed on, my muscles wincing in pain. The clouds circled the town like vultures waiting for my death, while the Sun shone brightly overhead, turning the sky into a glowing halo. I found my house in pristine condition, everything was back to where it was. No damage, no marks, nothing. At first, I thought I’d gone mad. Then I realized—this was my sentence. Time didn’t work here. The sun didn’t move, instead sitting still at zenith in the sky glaring at me like a warden judging prisoners for their misdeeds. The clock on my wall was stuck at 6:47 PM, the second hand trembling but never advancing. I walked through empty streets, calling out, but my voice just echoed back. The grocery store was fully stocked. Fresh bread, ripe fruit, milk that never soured. I took what I needed, and the next day, the shelves were full again. The faucets ran endlessly—clean, cold water, as if piped from some unseen reservoir. Time didn’t seem to move, but hunger pangs and thirst still gnawed at me, and the town provided. Like a jailer feeding its prisoner. Like a god taunting a sinner. I tried to leave once. Walking straight into the storm’s edge, where the wind screamed like a living thing, I pressed a palm up to the cloud. It was as soft as cotton, and I took a daring step forward, my arm sinking down to the elbow into the cloud. It felt warm, like water in a stagnant pool that had been sitting in the hot summer heat. Without warning, the clouds threw me back, launching me back several feet and I stumbled to the ground. "No", it seemed to whisper. "You stay." So I did. Days? Months? Years? No way to tell. The same sun hung in the same spot. The same clouds coiled above. I stopped counting. Stopped caring. I started keeping a diary of my time in this prison. It was filled up long ago, holding secrets the void had whispered to me in my sleep and the wisdom I gained by staring into the sky. I found the church unlocked, its steeple back in place as if nothing had ever happened. It was the only place I hadn’t gone yet. Maybe because I knew what waited for me there. The pews were empty, dustless. The stained glass threw fractured light across the floor. At the altar, a single candle burned, its flame unwavering. The wind beyond the town growled, low and eternal. I knelt. “Is this punishment?” I asked the silence. “Or forgiveness?” No answer. Just the distant rumble of the storm. I stayed there a long time. Sometimes, I’d hear voices—fragments of old conversations, carried on a breeze that didn’t exist. My wife’s laugh. My mother’s last words to me. I’d turn, and there’d be nothing. Just the town. Just the storm. Just me. I learned how to ignore them. The air was cool enough to be comfortable but not bone-chilling, and the light provided a source of comfort to me. If this was repentance, it was surprisingly tame. The loneliness crushed me at times, but I always managed to overcome it. I accepted my new life here long ago. One day the sky changed. As I strolled down the street walking to the grocery store, a single, tiny snowflake floated down slowly from the sky and landed at my feet. The first time in a long while clouds filled in the little hole at the top of the storm, blocking out the sun. Snow fell from the sky, coating everything in a blanket of white. I sat on the ground, letting the snow build up around me as I stared absently into space. There was only one thing I was sure of.
[[File:Itsnotsobadhere.png]]
It's not so bad here.
